summary: This brief post covers three topics: the importance of hiring testers for software teams, the usability challenges of multi-row tab dialogs, and a recommendation for Danny Goodman's Dynamic HTML reference book. Spolsky argues that not hiring testers is a false economy and links to a longer article on the subject. He acknowledges reader feedback that tab dialogs break down with multiple rows, and proposes an alternative design. He also enthusiastically endorses a comprehensive HTML reference as far superior to Microsoft's documentation.
key_points: Not hiring software testers is one of the worst false economy decisions a company can make
Tab dialogs are great for usability but break down badly when you need more than one row of tabs
If you need multiple rows of tabs, your design is probably too complicated and needs simplification
Comprehensive, well-tested cross-browser references are far more valuable than vendor documentation
money_quotes: Every software development team needs testers, but too many companies just won't hire them.
This is one of the worst 'false economy' moves you can make.
All the designs I've ever seen with multiple rows are terrible – they're either confusing, or they violate realism in some way.
You could make a good case that if you have to have more than one row of tabs, your design is too complicated and it needs to be simplified.
As soon as I started using this book instead of the shoddy, disorganized, unindexed 'documentation' that Microsoft provides, I became a significantly happier person.
key_insight: Software teams chronically underinvest in quality infrastructure — whether that means hiring testers or finding proper reference documentation — and these false economies cost far more than they save.
